serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of ajvar with bread is approximately 250 g, comprising a generous portion of roasted red pepper and eggplant dip alongside two to three slices of bread.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Roasted Red Pepper Spread / Vegetable Dip with Bread (composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ajvar with bread is not listed as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: roasted red peppers (FDC ID 170108), eggplant cooked (FDC ID 169228), olive oil (FDC ID 171413), garlic (FDC ID 169230), and white bread (FDC ID 353340), blended in proportions consistent with a typical ajvar-with-bread dish (approximately 40% ajvar dip, 60% bread by weight). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite estimate and found broadly consistent; input values are retained with medium confidence. Energy 180 kcal/100g is consistent with a bread-dominant mixed dish. Sodium 240 mg/100g reflects added salt in ajvar preparation plus bread sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — White bread and mixed vegetable dip analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ajvar with bread as a combined dish. GI estimated at 65 based on the dominant carbohydrate contributor being white bread (GI ~70–75 per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the high fibre (3.2 g/100g), fat (10 g/100g), and vegetable content of the ajvar component, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective GI of the meal. A blended GI of 65 (medium category) is defensible for this bread-dominant mixed dish. Glycemic load per 250 g serving: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 45.0) / 100 = 29.3 (high GL per serving due to large bread portion).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100g; 600 mg per 250 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g serving delivers approximately 600 mg sodium — 26% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association — so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt ajvar preparation, and choose wholegrain bread to benefit from the potassium and fibre content of the red pepper and eggplant base.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 18 g/100g; estimated GI 65; GL 29.3 per 250 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: The bread component drives a medium GI and high glycemic load per serving (GL ~29), which is likely to produce a meaningful postprandial glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute wholegrain or high-fibre bread to lower the GI, reduce the bread portion, and increase the ajvar-to-bread ratio to moderate the overall carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Olive oil provides predominantly monounsaturated fat (~6.2 g/100g); saturated fat low at 1.6 g/100g; no cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The olive oil base contributes a favourable monounsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio consistent with Mediterranean dietary patterns associated with reduced cardiovascular risk, and the red pepper and eggplant base provides fibre and vitamin C; however, the sodium content of 240 mg/100g warrants portion control for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetables (red bell pepper, eggplant) — solanine content may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Red bell peppers and eggplant are nightshade vegetables whose solanine alkaloids may trigger or worsen joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; while olive oil provides oleocanthal with anti-inflammatory properties and vitamin C (28 mg/100g) supports collagen synthesis, individuals with RA should monitor for symptom flares and consider nightshade elimination trials under dietitian supervision.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ajvar with bread is a Balkan dish consisting of ajvar — a richly flavoured roasted red pepper and eggplant dip — served alongside bread, typically white flatbread, pita, or sliced loaf. Ajvar originates from the Western Balkans, particularly Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Croatia, where it is a staple condiment and appetiser. The dip is prepared by roasting red bell peppers and eggplant until charred and tender, then blending them with garlic, olive oil, and salt to produce a smooth or slightly chunky spread with a sweet, smoky, and mildly tangy flavour profile. The bread component adds a soft, starchy base that balances the intensity of the dip. Per 100 g of the combined dish, the nutritional profile reflects the bread-dominant composition: approximately 180 kcal, 4 g protein, 10 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), 18 g carbohydrate, 3.2 g dietary fibre, 3.2 g sugar, and 240 mg sodium. Vitamin C content is notable at 28 mg/100g, contributed by the red bell pepper base. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, and halal, and contains gluten from the bread. Clinically, the primary concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes is the medium glycemic index (~65) and high glycemic load per serving (~29) driven by the bread component; substituting wholegrain bread and increasing the ajvar-to-bread ratio is recommended. For hypertension, sodium at 240 mg/100g is borderline and portion control is advised. For cardiovascular health, the olive oil base contributes a favourable monounsaturated fat profile consistent with Mediterranean dietary principles. Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should be aware that red bell peppers and eggplant are nightshade vegetables that may aggravate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
common_user_queries: What are the calories in ajvar with bread?

confidence_reason: Ajvar with bread is not listed as a single entry in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for roasted red peppers, eggplant, olive oil, garlic, and white bread in proportions consistent with a typical ajvar-with-bread dish. The input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI moderated by fibre and fat content. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries for roasted red peppers, eggplant, olive oil, garlic, and white bread; no single approved-source entry exists for ajvar with bread as a combined dish. Input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7) are retained as they are consistent with the USDA component estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (6.2) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.4) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central olive oil and vegetable component data; these are not directly reported in the input record. 3. potassium_mg (200.0) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central red pepper and eggplant data; not directly reported in the input record. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish (ajvar dip with bread in Balkan preparation style) is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2024; component-level data exists but reliable proportional blending for all micronutrients is not defensible without a dedicated food composition entry. 5. Cultural metadata correction: the input record incorrectly classified ajvar as Middle Eastern cuisine with ethnic tags of Shawarma and Kebab, and listed country_of_origin as Singapore. Ajvar is a Balkan dish originating from Serbia, North Macedonia, Bosnia and Herzegovina, and Croatia; these errors have been corrected in this output. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for ajvar with bread was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed as naturally zero for this plant-based olive-oil preparation with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed as naturally zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients.
